questions.question: In `sales/ajax_load.php`, the `save_so` action supports two submission modes from the UI. What happens when the order is submitted with 'created' status versus 'draft' status?

options.text: Both modes create the order but 'created' sends an email notification while 'draft' does not

'Created' status calls `$stockist->update_inventory_status($uid, 'sales')` + `$stockist->add_inventory_log($uid, 'sales', 'Offline Sales :: SO_XXXXXX')` to deduct inventory per UID, while 'draft' status creates a Picklist via `new Picklist(); $picklist->create_picklist('offline', $result)` without inventory deduction
'Draft' saves to a temporary table and 'created' saves to TBL_SALE_ORDER
'Created' requires manager approval while 'draft' saves immediately

questions.question: What database transaction mechanism does the `save_so` action use, and what is its limitation?

options.text: It uses MySQL SAVEPOINT for nested transactions with rollback to each savepoint on failure

It uses `$db->startTransaction()` and `$db->commit()` but has no explicit `$db->rollback()` on failure — if $stockist inventory updates succeed for some UIDs but a subsequent step fails, the inventory changes are not rolled back
It uses no transactions — each database write is independent and auto-committed
It uses SELECT FOR UPDATE to lock the inventory rows before updating them

questions.question: How does the `save_so` action store the cost price for each line item, and what underlying method does it use?

options.text: It reads the `cost_price` column directly from `TBL_PRODUCTS_MASTER`
It stores `item_cp` via `get_cost_price('pid', $item['item_id'])` which internally calls `$stockist->get_current_acp()` — the average cost price from the inventory system
It calculates cost price as a fixed percentage (70%) of the selling price
It retrieves cost price from the most recent purchase order in `TBL_PURCHASE_ORDERS`

questions.question: In the `save_sor` (sales return) action, what validation does the code perform on each UID before processing the return?

options.text: It checks that the UID belongs to an order that was placed within the last 30 days
It verifies that the UID exists in `TBL_INVENTORY` with `inv_status = 'sales'` — only items currently in 'sales' status can be returned
It validates the UID against the product's warranty expiration date in `TBL_REGISTERED_WARRANTY`
It checks that the UID has a valid barcode format matching the product's SKU pattern

questions.question: When `save_sor` processes a valid return UID, what `$stockist` methods are called and what side effects does the `sales_return` log type trigger?

options.text: Only `$stockist->update_inventory_status($uid, 'qc_verified')` — the item goes directly back to sellable stock

`$stockist->update_inventory_status($uid, 'qc_pending')` + `$stockist->add_inventory_log($uid, 'sales_return', 'Sales Return :: SO_XXXXXX')` — the 'sales_return' log type triggers `expectedLocation='RETURN_00001'`, `inv_batch=today`, `ctn_id='2002'`, and warranty deactivation in `TBL_REGISTERED_WARRANTY`
`$stockist->update_inventory_status($uid, 'inbound')` + `$stockist->add_inventory_log($uid, 'return', ...)` — the item re-enters the inbound pipeline
Only `$stockist->add_inventory_log($uid, 'sales_return', ...)` — the status is not changed, only logged

questions.question: How does the `save_payment` action in `sales/ajax_load.php` handle cash sale payments differently from party-based payments?

options.text: Cash sale payments use a different payment gateway (Razorpay) while party payments use bank transfers

Party payments (party_id > 0) insert into `TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS`, while cash sale payments (party_id = 0) update `TBL_SALE_ORDER.so_notes` JSON field with `payment_status: 'paid'` or `'partial_paid'` based on whether the payment covers the full SO total
Cash sale payments are stored in `TBL_CASH_PAYMENTS` (a separate table), while party payments go to `TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS`
Both paths are identical — they both insert into `TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS` regardless of party_id

questions.question: How does the `get_cash_ledger` action differ from the standard `get_customer_ledger` action in terms of financial calculation?

options.text: Both delegate to `$accountant->get_party_leadger()` with different party types

`get_customer_ledger` delegates to `$accountant->get_party_leadger('customer', ...)`, while `get_cash_ledger` implements its own custom ledger — directly querying `TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS` and `TBL_SALE_RETURN_ITEMS`, merging both result sets, sorting by date, and calculating running balance from `so_total_amount` — bypassing the `$accountant` system entirely
`get_cash_ledger` only shows payments while `get_customer_ledger` also shows order totals
`get_cash_ledger` uses real-time Razorpay API calls while `get_customer_ledger` reads from local tables

questions.question: What role flag architecture does `TBL_PARTIES` use, and how do the three Parties UI pages expose different role combinations?

options.text: Each party type has its own separate table (TBL_CUSTOMERS, TBL_SUPPLIERS, TBL_VENDORS) linked by party_id

TBL_PARTIES uses boolean flag columns (`party_customer`, `party_supplier`, `party_carrier`, `party_distributor`, `party_ams`) — `customers.php` exposes Distributor + Customer checkboxes, `suppliers.php` exposes Supplier + Carrier checkboxes, `vendors.php` exposes Supplier + AMS Vendor checkboxes — allowing simultaneous multi-role assignment in a single party row
A single `party_type` ENUM column with values 'customer', 'supplier', 'carrier', 'distributor', 'ams'
A junction table `TBL_PARTY_ROLES` maps party_id to role_id for flexible role assignment

questions.question: What happens in the `update_parties` action when `party_distributor` is toggled to `1` for a party?

options.text: Only the `party_distributor` flag is updated in `TBL_PARTIES` — no other side effects

A cascade is triggered: `$brandRegulator->generateDistTNC($party_id)` generates a Terms & Conditions PDF, `$notification->send()` dispatches an onboarding email, and JSON fields `party_approval_status` and `party_status` are updated with `'signed_docs_pending'` status
The party is automatically added to all marketplace seller accounts (Flipkart, Amazon, Shopify)
A new database table `TBL_SALE_ORDER_{party_id}` is created for the distributor's dedicated orders

questions.question: In the AMS invoice creation chain, what tables does `$accountant->get_ams_orders()` read from to aggregate vendor order data for commission calculation?

options.text: Only `TBL_AMS_INVOICES` and `TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS` — previous AMS invoices are used to derive current orders

`TBL_FK_ORDERS`, `TBL_AZ_ORDERS`, `TBL_FK_RETURNS`, `TBL_AZ_RETURNS` (order and return data) joined with `TBL_FK_ACCOUNTS`, `TBL_AZ_ACCOUNTS` (account→party mapping) — reading from 6 external marketplace-owned tables
`TBL_SALE_ORDER` and `TBL_SALE_RETURN_ITEMS` — using offline sales data for commission calculation
A pre-aggregated `TBL_AMS_TRANSACTIONS` table updated by a nightly cron job

questions.question: How does `$accountant->get_ams_slab_rate()` determine the applicable commission rate for a specific vendor transaction?

options.text: It reads a flat rate directly from `TBL_PARTIES.party_ams_rates` — a single numeric value per vendor

It reads `TBL_PARTIES.party_ams_rate_slab` (JSON array of `{slab_id, startDate, endDate}` objects) to find the active slab by date, then reads `TBL_AMS_RATE_SLABS.slab_card` JSON for rate configuration — supporting percentage commission_fees and flat or variable fixed_fees with GST including→excluding conversion at 18/118 ratio
It calculates the rate dynamically based on the vendor's total monthly order volume divided by revenue
It uses a hardcoded rate table defined in `config.php` that applies to all vendors equally

questions.question: How does the AMS `create_ams_invoice()` method handle invoice numbering for credit notes versus regular invoices?

options.text: Credit notes use the same numbering sequence as invoices — there is no distinction

Regular invoices use `{FIRM}-{FY}-AMS-{XXXX}` format while credit notes (generated when total is negative) use `{FIRM}-{FY}-AMS-CN{XXXX}` format — both sequentially numbered per firm_id from `TBL_OPTIONS`
Credit notes are stored as negative-amount entries in the same invoice record
Credit notes are generated as separate PDF files but do not appear in `TBL_AMS_INVOICES`

questions.question: In `ams/ajax_load.php`, what specific table ownership violation does the `delete_payment` action commit?

options.text: It deletes from `TBL_AMS_INVOICES` instead of `TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS` — removing invoice records instead of payment records

It executes `$db->where('ams_payment_id', $payment_id); $db->delete(TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS)` — targeting the Sales-owned `bas_sale_payments` table (PK: `sales_payment_id`) instead of the AMS-owned `TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS` (PK: `ams_payment_id`), while `save_payment` in the same file correctly targets `TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS`
It deletes from `TBL_PARTIES` — removing the vendor party record instead of the payment
It uses `$db->delete(TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS)` but with the wrong WHERE clause column `sales_payment_id`

questions.question: What are the two distinct problems with the AMS `delete_payment` action's database operation?

options.text: Performance: it scans the full table, and it locks the table during deletion

(1) **Wrong table**: `TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS` (Sales-owned, physical: `bas_sale_payments`) is targeted instead of `TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS` (AMS-owned, physical: `bas_ams_payments`). (2) **Wrong column**: the WHERE clause uses `ams_payment_id` which is the PK of `TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS`, not a column in `TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS` (whose PK is `sales_payment_id`)
(1) Missing transaction wrapper, (2) No soft-delete mechanism
(1) The delete is not logged to an audit table, (2) No notification is sent to the vendor

questions.question: What are the three party type branches in `$accountant->get_current_dues()`, and what formula does each use?

options.text: All three use the same formula: total orders minus total payments

'customer': `SUM(TBL_SALE_ORDER.so_total_amount) + old_system_dues(osd) − SUM(TBL_SALE_RETURN_ITEMS.return_amount) − SUM(TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS.payment_amount)`. 'supplier': hardcoded return 0 (not implemented). 'vendor': `SUM(TBL_AMS_INVOICES.ams_invoice_total) − SUM(TBL_AMS_PAYMENTS.payment_amount)`
'customer': reads from a pre-calculated `balance` column in TBL_PARTIES. 'supplier': sums TBL_PURCHASE_ORDERS. 'vendor': sums TBL_AMS_RATE_SLABS
All three query a unified `TBL_FINANCIAL_LEDGER` table with different `ledger_type` filters

questions.question: How does `$accountant->get_party_leadger()` generate a unified ledger for a customer, and how does it calculate the running balance?

options.text: It reads from a pre-sorted `TBL_LEDGER_ENTRIES` table and uses the stored running balance column

It fetches transactions from `get_all_transactions()` for 3 types ('sales', 'sales_return', 'sales_payment'), merges all into a single array, sorts by date, then calculates running balance **backward** from the current dues — starting from `get_current_dues('customer')` and working backward through the sorted transactions to produce a Balance C/F for date-range-truncated outputs
It simply queries TBL_SALE_PAYMENTS ordered by date and displays the payment_amount as the running balance
It calls the $stockist class to get inventory-based financial snapshots for each transaction date

questions.question: How many modules call `$accountant` methods, and what is the approximate distribution of calls?

options.text: Only the AMS module calls $accountant — 11 calls for invoice and payment management

Three modules: Sales (7 calls: `get_party_leadger('customer')`, `get_current_dues('customer')`, `get_all_transactions`), AMS (11 calls: `create_ams_invoice`, `create_ams_invoice_csv`, `generate_tax_invoice_pdf`, `send_mail_notification`, `get_party_leadger('vendor')`, `get_current_dues('vendor')`, `get_all_transactions`), and Parties (3 calls: `get_current_dues('customer'/'supplier'/'ams')`) — totalling 21 calls
All six marketplace modules plus Sales and AMS — approximately 50 calls
Only Sales and Parties — AMS has its own financial calculation class

questions.question: What makes `TBL_PARTIES` the most interconnected table in the FIMS system, and what risk does its multi-role architecture create?

options.text: It stores financial transaction data that all modules read for reconciliation

It is referenced by 50+ locations across 9+ modules (Sales, AMS, Purchase, Distributor, Flipkart, Amazon, Inventory, Tools, monthly_report). Its boolean role flag model means a single row can simultaneously be customer + supplier + distributor + AMS vendor. AMS-specific JSON fields (`party_ams_rate_slab`) and distributor-specific JSON fields (`party_approval_status`, `party_status`) coexist in each row — meaning corruption or inconsistency in one party row propagates to all dependent modules simultaneously
It is the only table that uses foreign key constraints, making it central to referential integrity
It stores user credentials that all modules use for authentication
